posts.text: AI governance is pulling us in 5 directions at once: centralized vs. decentralized, robust vs. minimal enforcement, adaptive vs. enduring rules. My new work explores how aviation, climate, and open-source models might guide us through this complexity 🧵 [LINK]

The challenge: balancing competing tensions in AI regulation

• Centralized vs. decentralized control
• Robust vs. minimal enforcement  
• Adaptive vs. enduring rules
• Private leadership vs. shared governance
• Narrow vs. broad societal goals

I adapt lessons from 4 existing governance models:

✈️ Aviation: Centralized, safety-first, but costly
🌱 Organic food: Decentralized, private-driven, inconsistent
🌍 Climate: Shared, flexible, but weak enforcement
💻 Open-source: Collaborative, adaptive, but fragile

Current reality: fragmentation everywhere

• EU centralizing with AI Act; US taking decentralized approach
• Monitoring ranges from strict compliance to voluntary audits
• Tech firms dominate decisions while public input gets sidelined
The wildcard? AI's pace itself. Generative AI caught policymakers off guard. A major crisis—deepfake election scandal—could force rapid regulation. Meanwhile, public demand for accountability grows 📈

The path forward suggests adaptive, hybrid models combining:

• Centralized safety oversight (aviation-style)
• Decentralized innovation hubs (open-source style)
• Broad societal goals (climate-style)

Experimentation will be key 🔬
Why this matters: AI governance shapes society itself. Done right, it addresses inequality and climate impact. Done wrong, it entrenches power imbalances and amplifies harms. The stakes are enormous

For policy practitioners and governance teams: What trade-offs would you prioritize? Centralization or flexibility? Private leadership or public control?

Paper: [LINK] @GRAIL_center @purduepolsci #AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI #TechPolicy

linkedin.text:
AI governance is pulling us in five directions at once, and we need better frameworks to navigate this complexity. My new research explores how lessons from aviation, climate policy, and open-source software might guide AI regulation. [LINK]

✅ The core challenge: AI regulation faces fundamental tensions between centralized vs. decentralized control, robust vs. minimal enforcement, adaptive vs. enduring rules, private leadership vs. shared governance, and narrow vs. broad societal goals.

➤ I analyze four existing governance models for insights:
• Aviation: Centralized, safety-first approach but costly to implement
• Organic food: Decentralized, private-driven but creates inconsistencies  
• Climate policy: Shared governance, flexible but weak enforcement
• Open-source software: Collaborative, adaptive but potentially fragile

• Current reality shows fragmentation everywhere. The EU is centralizing with the AI Act while the US takes a decentralized, sector-specific approach. Monitoring ranges from strict compliance to voluntary audits, and tech firms dominate decisions while public input often gets sidelined.

➤ The path forward suggests adaptive, hybrid models that combine centralized safety oversight (like aviation), decentralized innovation hubs (like open-source), and broad societal goals (like climate policy). Experimentation will be essential.

Why this matters for policy practitioners and governance teams: AI governance will shape not just technology but society itself. Done right, it can address inequality and climate impact. Done wrong, it risks entrenching power imbalances.

GRAIL Center | Purdue University

posts.text: AI governance faces fundamental tensions: centralized vs. decentralized control, robust vs. minimal enforcement, adaptive vs. enduring rules. My new work explores how aviation, climate, and open-source models might guide us 🧵 [LINK]

The challenge: balancing competing forces in AI regulation

• Centralized vs. decentralized control
• Robust vs. minimal enforcement
• Adaptive vs. enduring rules  
• Private leadership vs. shared governance
• Narrow vs. broad societal goals

I examine 4 governance models for lessons:

✈️ Aviation: Centralized, safety-first, costly
🌱 Organic food: Decentralized, private-driven, inconsistent
🌍 Climate: Shared, flexible, weak enforcement
💻 Open-source: Collaborative, adaptive, fragile
Today's reality: fragmentation dominates. EU centralizing with AI Act; US going decentralized. Monitoring varies from strict compliance to voluntary audits. Tech firms lead while public input lags
The wildcard: AI's breakneck pace. Generative AI blindsided policymakers. A crisis—like deepfake election interference—could trigger rapid regulation. Public accountability demands intensify

The way forward: adaptive, hybrid models combining centralized safety oversight (aviation-style), decentralized innovation (open-source), and broad societal goals (climate-style). Experimentation essential

For policy practitioners: What trade-offs matter most? Centralization or flexibility? Private leadership or public control? AI governance shapes society itself—the stakes couldn't be higher
